title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
What is Insurable Interest in Life Insurance?
Insurance contracts are governed by unique principles that ensure that the insurance policy is bought only for compensation for a loss. The policyholder should not make a profit from the policy or buy the policy with a fraudulent intention.
While there are three important principles governing insurance contracts, the principle of insurable interest is an important criterion. Let’s understand what the principle is all about.
What is Insurable Interest in Life Insurance?
Insurable interest, in simple terms, means a financial interest in the subject being insured, i.e., the policyholder would suffer a financial loss if the insured subject suffers any contingency.
In a life insurance policy, the insured subject is an individual on whose life the policy is bought. So, under life insurance plans, insurable interest is said to exist if the policyholder suffers a financial loss if the insured individual dies.
If you buy term insurance or other life insurance policy for yourself, you have an unlimited insurable interest in your life because, in the case of your demise, you would lose your financial ability to earn. So, every individual is said to have an unlimited insurable interest in his own life.
However, when buying life insurance plans for others, i.e., another life is being insured while you are the policyholder, insurable interest has to exist. In such cases, the principle of insurable interest has to be fulfilled. The insurer checks the application of the insurable interest before issuing the policy1. Insurable interest exists both in life as well as general insurance. However, it needs to be established only at the time of policy inception in Life Insurance1.
Eligibility to have Insurable Interest in Life Insurance
In a life insurance policy, the following relations are deemed eligible to have an insurable interest.
An individual in his own life.
A married couple on the life of the other. While the wife loses financially if the husband passes away prematurely, the husband also suffers a financial loss in the absence of his wife.
Parents on the life of their children, biological or adopted. In this case, the insurable interest is considered on the emotional bond that parents have with their children.
Grandparents on the lives of their grandchildren.
Employers on the lives of their employees because if the employee dies, the employer would suffer a financial loss in replacing him.
Lenders on the lives of their borrowers because if the borrower dies, the lender would lose the amount loaned. In this case, insurable interest exists up to the amount of the loan.
Besides these relations, insurable interest is not said to exist between other individuals.
When does Insurable Interest not Exist?
Common examples of relations, wherein insurable interest does not exist, are as follows –
Children have no insurable interest in their parents' lives. This is because once children become financially independent, they don’t suffer any financial loss if the parent passes away. The concept of emotional bonding is not applicable in this case. So, while parents can buy an insurance policy for their children, children can’t do the same for their parents.
Siblings don’t have any insurable interest in each other’s lives. As such, a brother or a sister cannot buy a life insurance policy for the other sibling.
No insurable interest exists between cousins, uncles and aunts, grandparents, or foster children.
The Principle of Insurable Interest in Life Insurance
In life insurance policies, insurable interest is necessary. If you are buying a policy on your own life, you don’t have to prove any insurable interest. However, if you are paying for the coverage of another individual, the relationship has to be established and there must be an insurable interest in such a relationship.
That being said, the insurable interest should exist at the time of buying the term life insurance or any other type of policy. Later on, the policy would continue even if the insurable interest ceases to apply. The common example is in the case of a married couple. If the husband buys the policy on the life of his wife or vice-versa, the insurable interest is assessed at the time of buying the policy. This means the insurer would check for a legal marital relationship only when issuing the policy. If, after the issuance of the policy, the couple gets divorced, it does not matter to the insurance company. The policy would continue even when the couple is not legally married anymore.
Conclusion
Insurable interest is an important principle in insurance policies, both general and life insurance policies. However, the application of the principle differs in both plans. So, if you are buying a term life insurance plan for someone else, understand the concept of insurable interest. Ensure that you have an insurable interest on the person being insured so that the term insurance policy can be issued.
